abstract: Local feature matching has long been a fundamental component of 3D vision systems such as Structure-from-Motion (SfM), yet progress has lagged behind the rapid advances of modern data-driven approaches. The newer approaches, such as feed-forward reconstruction models, have benefited extensively from scaling dataset sizes, whereas local feature matching models are still only trained on a few mid-sized datasets. In this paper, we revisit local feature matching from a data-driven perspective. In our approach, which we call LoMa, we combine large and diverse data mixtures, modern training recipes, scaled model capacity, and scaled compute, resulting in remarkable gains in performance. Since current standard benchmarks mainly rely on collecting sparse views from successful 3D reconstructions, the evaluation of progress in feature matching has been limited to relatively easy image pairs. To address the resulting saturation of benchmarks, we collect 1000 highly challenging image pairs from internet data into a new dataset called HardMatch. Ground truth correspondences for HardMatch are obtained via manual annotation by the authors. In our extensive benchmarking suite, we find that LoMa makes outstanding progress across the board, outperforming the state-of-the-art method ALIKED+LightGlue by +18.6 mAA on HardMatch, +29.5 mAA on WxBS, +21.4 (1m, 10$^\circ$) on InLoc, +24.2 AUC on RUBIK, and +12.4 mAA on IMC 2022. We release our code and models publicly at this https URL.

citations.fullCitation: Lindenberger, P., Sarlin, P.E., Pollefeys, M.: LightGlue: Local Feature Matching at Light Speed. In: ICCV (2023) 2, 3, 4, 6, 12, 9
citations.justification: LoMa's matcher component is directly based on the LightGlue architecture. The paper's improvements are demonstrated by applying a novel large-scale training strategy to this foundational model, making it a critical architectural reference.
citations.title: LightGlue: Local Feature Matching at Light Speed

citations.fullCitation: Edstedt, J., Bökman, G., Wadenbäck, M., Felsberg, M.: DeDoDe: Detect, Don’t Describe – Describe, Don’t Detect for Local Feature Matching. In: 3dv (2024) 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 12
citations.justification: LoMa utilizes the DeDoDe architecture for its feature descriptor. The paper highlights that its novel data-driven training approach significantly enhances the performance of this existing descriptor, forming a core part of the LoMa pipeline.
citations.title: DeDoDe: Detect, Don’t Describe – Describe, Don’t Detect for Local Feature Matching

citations.fullCitation: Zhao, X., Wu, X., Chen, W., Chen, P.C.Y., Xu, Q., Li, Z.: Aliked: A lighter keypoint and descriptor extraction network via deformable transformation. IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation & Measurement 72 (2023) 1, 3, 5, 12, 9
citations.justification: The combination of ALIKED and LightGlue is consistently used throughout the paper as the primary state-of-the-art sparse matching baseline. LoMa's superior performance is established by directly outperforming this method, making it the key competitor to surpass.
citations.title: Aliked: A lighter keypoint and descriptor extraction network via deformable transformation

citations.fullCitation: Sun, J., Shen, Z., Wang, Y., Bao, H., Zhou, X.: LoFTR: Detector-free local feature matching with transformers. In: CVPR (2021) 2, 4, 10, 12, 1, 9
citations.justification: This paper introduced the popular detector-free matching paradigm, which the LoMa paper explicitly challenges. LoMa's central argument is to revive and advance traditional detector-based methods, positioning its work as a direct counterpoint to the trend initiated by LoFTR.
citations.title: LoFTR: Detector-free local feature matching with transformers

citations.fullCitation: Mishkin, D., Matas, J., Perdoch, M., Lenc, K.: WxBS: Wide baseline stereo generalizations. In: BMVC (2015) 3, 4, 10, 12, 5
citations.justification: The creation of the new HardMatch benchmark is a major contribution of the LoMa paper. The authors explicitly cite WxBS as the inspiration for their benchmark's design, which focuses on challenging, manually annotated image pairs, but notes that LoMa's dataset is significantly larger and more difficult.
citations.title: WxBS: Wide baseline stereo generalizations

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "LoMa: Local Feature Matching Revisited," covering its context, objectives, methodology, findings, and implications for the field of 3D vision.

---

## Research Report: LoMa: Local Feature Matching Revisited

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by a collaborative team from several institutions:
*   David Nordström and Fredrik Kahl are affiliated with Chalmers University of Technology.
*   Johan Edstedt, Mårten Wadenbäck, and Michael Felsberg are affiliated with Linköping University.
*   Georg Bökman is affiliated with the University of Amsterdam.
*   Jonathan Astermark, Anders Heyden, and Viktor Larsson are affiliated with the Centre for Mathematical Sciences, Lund University.
David Nordström and Johan Edstedt are noted as having contributed equally.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Local feature matching has historically served as a foundational component in 3D vision systems, such as Structure-from-Motion (SfM), which aims to reconstruct 3D environments from unordered image collections. Traditional approaches like SIFT [33] have been central to this paradigm. More recently, neural network models, including those for keypoint detection and description (e.g., SuperPoint [12], ALIKED [68], DeDoDe [16]) and sparse matching (e.g., SuperGlue [46], LightGlue [32]), have been introduced to enhance robustness and accuracy.

However, the field of local feature matching has recently experienced a shift in research focus, primarily due to the emergence of alternative paradigms. Detector-free methods, such as LoFTR [50] and RoMa [19], and feed-forward reconstruction models, including MASt3R [29] and VGGT [60], have gained prominence. These newer approaches have significantly benefited from the availability of increasingly large and diverse training datasets, often scaling to orders of magnitude more data than traditionally used for local feature matching models. A common argument in the context of detector-free methods has been that detector-based local feature matching possesses inherent limitations, leading to substantial research efforts in scaling detector-free SfM [13, 20, 24, 28] to overcome these perceived constraints.

The authors of "LoMa" contend that the perceived limitations and diminishing relevance of local feature matching may be attributable to a lack of investment in modern data-driven approaches, rather than fundamental deficiencies in the paradigm itself. This work re-examines local feature matching by applying contemporary machine learning practices, including large-scale data mixtures, advanced training recipes, and increased model capacity and computational resources. The paper seeks to demonstrate that, when afforded these modern developments, local feature matching can achieve performance levels competitive with or exceeding recent dense matching and feed-forward reconstruction pipelines, thereby re-establishing its position in the research landscape.

Furthermore, the paper addresses a critical issue in the evaluation of feature matching algorithms: benchmark saturation. Existing standard benchmarks, often derived from successful 3D reconstructions (e.g., MegaDepth [30], ScanNet [11]), primarily comprise relatively straightforward image pairs that modern state-of-the-art matchers can already solve with high accuracy. This saturation limits the ability to meaningfully assess progress, identify remaining failure modes, and guide future research. The introduction of the "HardMatch" dataset is a direct response to this limitation, aiming to provide a more challenging and diverse evaluation platform for assessing advancements in feature matching capabilities.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to revisit and advance the field of local feature matching by applying a modern, data-driven perspective. The authors are motivated by the observation that local feature matching has lagged behind other computer vision areas that have significantly benefited from increased data scale, model capacity, and computational resources.

The key objectives and motivations underpinning this work are detailed as follows:

1.  **Re-evaluate Local Feature Matching with Modern Data-Driven Techniques:** A core motivation is to challenge the prevailing view that local feature matching is inherently limited. The authors hypothesize that by adopting modern training methodologies, similar to those employed by detector-free and feed-forward reconstruction models, local feature matching can achieve substantial performance improvements. This involves integrating large and diverse data mixtures, modern training recipes, scaled model capacity, and increased compute.

2.  **Curate Large and Diverse Training Data:** Previous local feature matching models have often been trained on a limited number of mid-sized datasets (e.g., MegaDepth). The authors aim to significantly expand the training data by curating a diverse collection of 17 3D datasets, encompassing wide baseline and optical flow scenarios. This includes re-processing existing datasets (e.g., MegaDepth (Re-MVS), MegaScenes) and developing custom processing pipelines for others (e.g., SpatialVID) to generate high-quality ground truth. The motivation is to enhance model generalization and robustness across varied and challenging real-world conditions.

3.  **Develop Scalable Training Recipes:** Beyond data volume, the research seeks to optimize training protocols for both descriptors and matchers. This includes exploring hyperparameters, optimizers, and learning rate schedules to effectively leverage the large datasets and model capacities, ensuring efficient and effective learning.

4.  **Increase Model Capacity and Training Compute:** The authors aim to investigate the impact of larger neural network architectures (increased number of parameters) and greater computational resources on local feature matching performance. This objective is driven by the success of scaling models in other deep learning domains and its potential to unlock higher levels of accuracy and robustness.

5.  **Address Benchmark Saturation with a Challenging New Dataset:** Existing benchmarks are identified as being near saturation, making it difficult to discern meaningful progress in feature matching. To counteract this, a significant objective is the creation of "HardMatch," a novel dataset comprising 1000 highly challenging, manually annotated image pairs. This dataset is designed to provide a more discriminative evaluation platform for assessing model capabilities, especially in extreme matching scenarios. Its purpose is to clearly measure advancements, identify remaining failure modes, and guide future research directions beyond the limitations of current benchmarks.

6.  **Develop a Family of High-Performance Local Feature Models (LoMa):** A concrete objective is to develop and release a family of descriptor-matcher models, named LoMa, that demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of benchmarks. These models are intended to serve as strong baselines for future research, offering a balance of speed and accuracy.

In summary, the motivation is to revitalize local feature matching by demonstrating that with modern, scaled, and data-driven approaches, it remains a highly effective and competitive solution for fundamental 3D vision tasks.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The LoMa approach re-examines local feature matching through a data-driven lens, primarily focusing on advanced training strategies and data scaling, rather than introducing novel architectural components. The methodology is structured around a three-stage pipeline: keypoint detection, local feature description, and sparse matching.

**4.1. Pipeline Components and Architecture**

*   **Keypoint Detection:** The authors do not train a new keypoint detector. Instead, they leverage the DaD [17] detector to supervise both the descriptor and matcher training. An ablation study in the supplementary material compares DaD's performance against other detectors like DeDoDe v2 [15], DISK [55], and ALIKED [68], finding DaD to be robust.
*   **Descriptor:** The descriptor component is based on the DeDoDe [16] architecture. It processes an image and its detected keypoints to generate high-dimensional local feature representations. The descriptor is trained first.
*   **Matcher:** The matcher component is based on the LightGlue [32] architecture. It takes the keypoints and initial descriptions from two images and refines these descriptions through iterative self- and cross-attention. This refinement allows the matcher to establish correspondences by considering contextual information from both images. The matcher is trained second, with the descriptor frozen.

**4.2. Learning Objectives**

*   **Description Training:** For training the descriptor ($g_{\theta}$), the methodology follows DeDoDe [16] by employing a dual-softmax based loss. This loss encourages the similarity matrix of descriptions to align with ground truth matches, such that ground truth correspondences are maxima along both row and column dimensions of the dual-softmax matrix.
*   **Matching Training:** The matcher ($h_{\phi}$) training adheres to the LightGlue [32] approach. It refines features layer-wise using the dual-softmax loss on the refined features, along with an additional matchability loss. The matchability loss supervises a predicted matchability score for each keypoint using binary cross-entropy, where a keypoint is deemed matchable if it has a ground truth correspondence. Layer-wise supervision supports trade-offs between performance and inference speed.
*   **Ground Truth:** Ground truth correspondences for training are derived from known relative poses and depth maps within the various training datasets. A fixed number of 2048 keypoints per image are used during training.

**4.3. Architectural Variants**

The LoMa family includes three main variants: LoMa-B (Base), LoMa-L (Large), and LoMa-G (Gigantic). These variants share the same core architecture, comprising 9 transformer blocks that alternate between self-attention and cross-attention layers, with attention heads of dimension 64. They differ in their embedding dimensionality, which is 256, 512, and 1024 respectively. An additional lightweight variant, LoMa-B$_{128}$, uses the DeDoDe-B descriptor with a descriptor dimension of 128. Rotary position embeddings (RoPE) [49] are incorporated into self-attention layers.

**4.4. Training Data and Recipes**

A critical aspect of the LoMa methodology is the use of a large and diverse data mixture, significantly surpassing the training data scale of prior local feature matching methods. The training data mixture includes 17 3D datasets, combining wide baseline and optical flow data.
*   **Dataset Collection:** The mixture includes datasets such as ScanNet++ v2 [66], BlendedMVS [65], Map-Free [3], Hypersim [43], MegaScenes [54], MegaDepth [30], AerialMD [57], TartanAir v2 [62], Mapillary Planet-scale Depth [1], Aria Synthetic Environments [4], CO3Dv2 [41], MegaSynth [27], SpatialVID [59], FlyingThings3D [37], UnrealStereo4k [53], and Virtual KITTI 2 [9, 21].
*   **Data Augmentation:** For some datasets, the authors re-processed the data to generate higher-quality ground truth. For instance, COLMAP MVS was run on all MegaDepth scenes (MegaDepth (Re-MVS)) and a filtered subset of MegaScenes. For SpatialVID, a custom MVS pipeline was implemented using RoMa v2 correspondences and a PyTorch PatchMatch implementation to ensure sufficient accuracy.
*   **Training Parameters:** All models are trained using the AdamW [35] optimizer with a cosine annealing learning rate (peak of 2 × 10$^{-4}$), a global batch size of 64, a weight decay of 5 × 10$^{-5}$, and Exponential Moving Average (EMA) with a decay factor of 0.999. A fixed input resolution of 560 × 560 is used. The descriptor is trained for 50,000 steps, and the matcher for 250,000 steps.

**4.5. Inference and Matching Protocol**

During inference, refined descriptions from the final layer of the matcher are used to form a dual-softmax matrix $P_{ij}$. A correspondence is established if $P_{ij}$ represents a mutual maximum along both rows and columns, and if $P_{ij}$ exceeds a threshold of $\mu = 0.1$. For evaluations, 4096 keypoints are typically used, with supplementary analysis on varying keypoint counts.

**4.6. HardMatch Dataset Creation**

To address benchmark saturation, the authors created HardMatch, a novel and challenging image matching benchmark:
*   **Image Sourcing:** Images were scraped from 100 diverse categories on Wikimedia Commons under permissive licenses.
*   **Difficult Pair Identification:** A large pool of candidate images was filtered by sampling pairs and using RoMa v2's confidence map. Pairs where RoMa v2 was uncertain (confidence between 0.3 and 0.9) were selected, manually inspected, and classified as matchable or unmatchable until 10 difficult pairs per category were identified, totaling 1000 pairs.
*   **Manual Annotation:** Ground truth correspondences were manually annotated for each pair, with 8 to 28 salient matches per pair.
*   **Grouping:** The dataset is organized into 9 challenging groups: Viewpoint Change, Temporal Gap, Illumination, Nature, Aerial, Celestial, Seasonal Change, Doppelgänger, and Hand Drawing.
*   **Human Baseline:** A human baseline was established by having independent annotators verify keypoint matches for a subset of pairs, providing an estimate of human error distribution.
*   **Evaluation Protocol:** HardMatch is evaluated by estimating a Fundamental matrix (F) using correspondences from the matcher via MAGSAC [6,7] (inlier threshold $\tau = 0.25$ pixels). The Percentage Correct Keypoints (PCK) is computed based on the epipolar error of the ground truth correspondences relative to the estimated F, across various pixel error thresholds. Dynamic scenes (approximately 20 pairs) are excluded from F-based evaluation.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research paper presents a comprehensive set of evaluations demonstrating the performance of the LoMa family of models across various benchmarks.

**5.1. Extreme Matching Benchmarks**

*   **HardMatch:** LoMa-G achieved a score of 54.3 mAA@10px on the HardMatch dataset, outperforming the state-of-the-art sparse method ALIKED+LightGlue by 18.6 mAA. It also slightly surpassed the best dense matchers (e.g., RoMa v2 at 46.5 mAA) and feed-forward reconstruction methods. HardMatch proved challenging for all evaluated methods, with LoMa-G’s performance notably lower than on WxBS. Specific challenging subgroups included Doppelgängers, large viewpoint changes, aerial photographs, and star constellations. The human baseline indicated a higher accuracy, but it was not directly comparable due to different evaluation metrics.
*   **WxBS:** On the WxBS [38] benchmark, LoMa-G achieved a score of 73.4 mAA@10px, slightly outperforming the dense matcher RoMa (72.6 mAA). This result established LoMa-G as the top performer among both sparse and dense matchers on this benchmark.

**5.2. Relative Pose Estimation**

*   **MegaDepth-1500 [30, 50] and ScanNet-1500 [11, 46]:** LoMa models consistently showed superior performance compared to other sparse matchers. LoMa-L achieved a gain of 8.4 AUC@5° on MegaDepth and 12.9 AUC@5° on ScanNet, relative to the second-best sparse method. For instance, LoMa-L achieved 56.5 AUC@5° on MegaDepth and 29.3 AUC@5° on ScanNet, significantly higher than ALIKED+LightGlue (48.1 and 14.5, respectively).

**5.3. Visual Localization**

*   **Map-free Relocalization [3]:** LoMa-G achieved a precision of 68.9 VCRE<90px (AUC of 90.3), representing an approximate 20-point increase in precision compared to other sparse matchers like ALIKED+LightGlue (47.2 precision, 79.5 AUC).
*   **InLoc [51]:** Using the HLoc [45] pipeline, LoMa-G significantly outperformed other sparse matchers. On the DUC2 sub-dataset, LoMa-G achieved 73.3% correct localizations at (0.25m, 2°), a gain of over 20 points compared to the second-best sparse matcher (ALIKED+LightGlue at 35.9%).
*   **Oxford Day-and-Night [63]:** For visual localization under challenging lighting conditions, LoMa-G demonstrated a greater than 14-point increase in accuracy for night queries at the most narrow threshold (0.25m, 2°), compared to other sparse matchers. LoMa-G achieved 58.9% correct localizations for night queries at this threshold.

**5.4. Additional Matching Evaluations**

*   **RUBIK [34]:** LoMa-G achieved an AUC of 73.2% at 10° and 79.9% at 20°, outperforming other sparse matchers by approximately 24 points in both metrics.
*   **Image Matching Challenge 2022 [25]:** LoMa-G achieved a mean average accuracy (mAA) of 89.3%, setting a new state of the art for this benchmark. This performance surpassed other sparse matchers and also exceeded the 2022 competition winner (an ensemble of LoFTR, DKM, and SuperGlue, which scored 86.3) and RoMa (88.0).
*   **HPatches [5]:** The lightweight LoMa-B$_{128}$ variant achieved the highest score on HPatches.
*   **SatAst [18]:** LoMa models, while outperforming other sparse matchers, showed some struggle with large in-plane rotations and scale changes characteristic of this dataset.

**5.5. Analysis and Ablations**

*   **Ablation Studies (HardMatch Validation Set):**
    *   Replacing ALIKED with DaD+DeDoDe (descriptor) and LightGlue (matcher) yielded a 5.7 mAA improvement over the ALIKED+LightGlue baseline.
    *   Extending the training data for the matcher and then the descriptor to the full 17-dataset mixture provided further generalization gains of 9.1 mAA.
    *   Longer training steps (from 50K to 250K for the matcher) added a moderate 0.4 mAA.
    *   Scaling the matcher embedding dimension from 256 to 1024 (LoMa-B to LoMa-G) contributed an additional 1.4 mAA. These ablations indicate that both increased data scale and model capacity significantly contribute to performance.
*   **Throughput:** LoMa-B demonstrated similar runtime to LightGlue while producing more accurate matches. At a batch size of 16 and 2048 keypoints on an A100 GPU, LoMa-B could process hundreds of pairs per second. When processing single image pairs (batch size 1), LoMa-B with L=3 layers could achieve almost 300 pairs per second. The layer-wise loss allowed for a trade-off between accuracy and speed via early stopping.
*   **Scaling:** The validation loss on HardMatch showed significant reductions with both increased data scale (transitioning from MegaDepth to the full dataset mixture) and increased model capacity (embedding dimensions from 128 to 1024).

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

This research contributes to the field of 3D computer vision by re-evaluating and advancing local feature matching through modern, data-driven methodologies. Its significance and potential impact can be summarized as follows:

1.  **Reaffirmation of Local Feature Matching:** The work demonstrates that local feature matching, when equipped with large-scale and diverse training data, advanced training recipes, and scaled model capacity, can achieve performance levels that are competitive with, and in several challenging scenarios, superior to contemporary detector-free methods and feed-forward reconstruction pipelines. This challenges the recent narrative that local feature matching is fundamentally limited or outdated, suggesting that its perceived stagnation was largely due to a lack of modernized approaches rather than inherent architectural constraints.

2.  **Introduction of a Challenging Benchmark (HardMatch):** The creation and release of HardMatch address a critical gap in the evaluation of feature matching algorithms. By providing 1000 manually annotated, highly challenging image pairs across diverse and extreme conditions, HardMatch offers a more discriminative benchmark that is not saturated by current state-of-the-art methods. This dataset enables more meaningful assessment of progress, aids in identifying persistent failure modes, and will guide future research towards developing more robust and generalizable matching solutions.

3.  **Advancement in Downstream 3D Vision Tasks:** Improved local feature matching performance has direct positive implications for a wide array of downstream 3D vision applications, including Structure-from-Motion (SfM), visual localization, and 3D reconstruction. The enhanced robustness and accuracy of LoMa, particularly in challenging environments (e.g., extreme viewpoint changes, varying illumination, temporal gaps), can lead to more reliable and precise results in these applications, facilitating advancements in areas like autonomous navigation, augmented reality, and large-scale mapping.

4.  **Open-Source Contribution and Research Guidance:** The public release of code, models, and training recipes for LoMa provides valuable resources for the research community. This facilitates reproducibility, encourages further development, and allows other researchers to build upon the established baselines. The detailed ablation studies and scaling analysis offer insights into which factors (data diversity, model capacity, training duration) yield the most significant performance gains, providing clear directions for future research in local feature matching.

5.  **New Baseline for Performance:** The LoMa family of models establishes new state-of-the-art performance across numerous established benchmarks (e.g., WxBS, IMC 2022, InLoc, Oxford Day-and-Night, RUBIK) and the newly introduced HardMatch. These improved baselines raise the performance bar for the field and set new targets for future innovations in feature matching.

In conclusion, "LoMa" highlights the enduring potential of local feature matching, showcasing that with focused application of modern deep learning principles, this foundational computer vision task can continue to evolve and deliver high-performance solutions for complex real-world problems.
overview:
## Scaling Local Feature Matching for the Modern Era

Local feature matching is a cornerstone of computer vision, essential for tasks that involve relating two or more images of the same scene. For decades, this process has followed a standard pipeline: detecting interest points (keypoints), describing the local area around those points with a vector (descriptors), and then finding pairs of descriptors that look similar across images (matching). These correspondences are the foundation for building 3D models from photos (Structure-from-Motion) and determining the position of a camera in a known environment (visual localization).

In recent years, the field has seen a significant shift toward "detector-free" methods. These approaches skip the explicit keypoint detection step, instead performing dense matching across the entire image. This shift was largely driven by the observation that traditional detector-based methods seemed to struggle with extreme viewpoint changes, lighting variations, and repetitive patterns. Simultaneously, feed-forward reconstruction models have emerged, promising to infer 3D geometry directly without the need for traditional matching pipelines.

![LoMa Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04931v1/x2.png)
*Figure 1: An overview of the LoMa pipeline compared to prior work. By scaling both data diversity and model capacity, LoMa re-establishes the competitiveness of detector-based local feature matching.*

The research presented in "LoMa: Local Feature Matching Revisited" challenges the assumption that detector-based matching has reached its fundamental limits. The authors argue that the performance gap between detector-based and detector-free methods is not due to architectural flaws but rather a disparity in training scale. While detector-free models have benefited from massive, diverse datasets and high-capacity neural networks, local feature matchers have largely remained confined to smaller, more specialized datasets. LoMa seeks to bridge this gap by applying modern data-driven practices—including large-scale data mixtures and increased model capacity—to the traditional local feature matching paradigm.

## The LoMa Pipeline: Components and Architecture

The LoMa system follows a three-stage approach: detection, description, and matching. Rather than proposing entirely new architectures for these stages, the authors select high-performing existing modules and focus on how to train them more effectively at scale.

For the detection stage, the model utilizes the DaD detector. This component identifies points in an image that are likely to be found in other views of the same scene. These points serve as the anchors for the subsequent stages.

The description stage uses the DeDoDe architecture. A descriptor network, denoted as $g_{\theta}$, processes the image and the detected keypoints to produce high-dimensional vectors. These vectors are designed to be "discriminative," meaning they should be unique to the specific visual patch they represent, and "invariant," meaning they should look similar even if the camera moves or the lighting changes.

The final stage is the matching, which uses the LightGlue architecture. The matcher, denoted as $h_{\phi}$, takes the descriptors from two images, $I^A$ and $I^B$, and refines them using a series of transformer blocks. These blocks use self-attention to look at other points within the same image and cross-attention to look at points in the other image. This iterative process allows the model to understand the spatial context of each point, making the final matching decision much more robust.

The LoMa family includes several variants to accommodate different computational budgets:
- **LoMa-B (Base):** Uses an embedding dimension $d_{\text{emb}}$ of 256.
- **LoMa-L (Large):** Increases $d_{\text{emb}}$ to 512.
- **LoMa-G (Gigantic):** Uses a $d_{\text{emb}}$ of 1024.

All variants typically use 9 transformer layers, though the architecture supports early stopping at any layer $L$ to trade accuracy for speed. For example, using $L=3$ can significantly speed up inference while maintaining high performance.

## Large-Scale Data Mixture and Modern Training

The most significant contribution of LoMa is the scale and diversity of its training data. While previous models were often trained on a single dataset like MegaDepth, LoMa uses a mixture of 17 different 3D datasets. This collection includes indoor scenes (ScanNet++), outdoor landmarks (MegaDepth, MegaScenes), aerial imagery (AerialMD), and synthetic environments (TartanAir, Aria).

Curating this data required more than just gathering files. The researchers re-processed several datasets to generate higher-quality "ground truth" correspondences. For instance, in the SpatialVID dataset, they used advanced matching and reconstruction techniques to create more accurate depth and pose information than was originally available.

The training process itself was also modernized. The models were trained using a global batch size of 64 on $560 \times 560$ resolution images. The optimization used a learning rate of $2 \times 10^{-4}$ with a weight decay of $5 \times 10^{-5}$ and an exponential moving average (EMA) factor of $0.999$. By exposing the model to such a wide variety of environments and camera motions during training, LoMa learns to be far more generalizable than its predecessors.

## Addressing Benchmark Saturation with HardMatch

As feature matching models have improved, established benchmarks like MegaDepth have become "saturated." Most modern matchers can solve these datasets with nearly 100% accuracy, making it impossible to distinguish which methods are truly better or where they still fail.

To solve this, the authors introduced **HardMatch**, a new dataset consisting of 1000 manually annotated image pairs. These pairs were specifically selected to be difficult for current state-of-the-art models. The images were scraped from Wikimedia Commons and categorized into nine challenging groups:

![HardMatch Categories](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04931v1/x4.png)
*Figure 2: Examples from the HardMatch dataset, showcasing the diversity of challenges including extreme viewpoint changes, temporal gaps, and different artistic styles.*

1.  **Viewpoint Change:** Significant shifts in the camera's position or angle.
2.  **Temporal Gap:** Images of the same place taken years or even decades apart.
3.  **Illumination:** Day-to-night transitions or harsh shadows.
4.  **Nature:** Scenes dominated by trees, rocks, and water, which lack clear geometric structure.
5.  **Aerial:** High-altitude views from planes or satellites.
6.  **Celestial:** Night skies and star constellations.
7.  **Seasonal Change:** The same scene in winter (snow) versus summer.
8.  **Doppelgänger:** Pairs of different buildings or objects that look nearly identical.
9.  **Hand Drawing:** Matching a real-world photo to a sketch or painting.

By providing manual annotations (8 to 28 ground truth points per pair), HardMatch allows for a precise evaluation of matching accuracy even in cases where automated 3D reconstruction fails.

## Experimental Findings and Performance

The results of the LoMa models across various tasks demonstrate the effectiveness of the data-driven approach. 

### HardMatch and WxBS
On the new HardMatch benchmark, LoMa-G achieved a score of 54.3 mAA@10px. To put this in perspective, it outperformed the popular ALIKED+LightGlue combination by over 18 points. It even surpassed leading dense matchers like RoMa v2. On the Wide Baseline Stereo (WxBS) benchmark, LoMa-G reached 73.4 mAA, setting a new record.

### Visual Localization
Visual localization is the task of finding exactly where a camera is within a pre-mapped area. LoMa showed dramatic improvements in this area:
- **InLoc:** For indoor localization, LoMa-G achieved 73.3% accuracy at a strict threshold of $0.25\text{m}$ and $2^{\circ}$, a gain of more than 20 points over previous sparse matchers.
- **Oxford Day-and-Night:** In a benchmark designed to test day-to-night matching, LoMa-G improved night query accuracy by 14 points at the narrowest threshold.

### Structure-from-Motion
The models were also tested on the Image Matching Challenge 2022 and MegaDepth relative pose tasks. LoMa-G set a new state-of-the-art on the IMC 2022 benchmark with 89.3 mAA. On the RUBIK dataset, which tests matching across very wide viewpoints, LoMa-G outperformed others by 24 points at both $10^{\circ}$ and $20^{\circ}$ thresholds.

![Scaling Analysis](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04931v1/x25.png)
*Figure 3: An analysis of how model performance improves as we increase the embedding dimension (model capacity) and the amount of training data. The loss decreases consistently as we scale up.*

## Speed vs. Accuracy Trade-offs

One of the practical advantages of detector-based methods like LoMa is their efficiency. Because they only process a sparse set of keypoints (usually 2048 or 4096) rather than every pixel in the image, they are often much faster than dense methods. 

The LoMa models maintain this advantage. The LoMa-B variant can process hundreds of image pairs per second on a modern GPU. Because the training objective is applied to every layer of the transformer, users can choose to stop the matcher after only 3 or 6 layers instead of the full 9. This allows for a flexible trade-off: using more layers for maximum accuracy on difficult pairs, or fewer layers for high-speed processing on easier pairs.

## Significance and Future Directions

The LoMa project serves as a "revisitation" of a classic paradigm. It demonstrates that the traditional pipeline of detection, description, and matching is far from obsolete. By applying the same "scaling laws" that have revolutionized other areas of AI—more data, bigger models, and better training recipes—the authors have shown that sparse matching can meet or exceed the performance of much more complex dense systems.

The introduction of HardMatch also provides the community with a vital tool for measuring future progress. It shifts the focus from "solved" benchmarks toward the extreme edge cases that still pose a challenge for computer vision. By making the code, models, and dataset publicly available, the researchers have provided a new high-performance baseline for 3D vision tasks.

Ultimately, LoMa suggests that the future of 3D reconstruction and localization may not lie in abandoning keypoint detectors, but in teaching them to handle the vast complexity of the real world through massive, diverse data. Establishing correspondences is established if the following dual-softmax condition is met:

$$P_{ij} = \max_k P_{ik} = \max_l P_{lj} \text{ and } P_{ij} > \mu$$

where $\mu = 0.1$ is the confidence threshold. This simple logic, backed by powerful data-driven features, remains a highly effective way to link images across time, space, and style.

keyInsights: Local feature matching, when modernized with sufficient data scale and model capacity, can achieve performance competitive with or superior to recent detector-free methods and feed-forward reconstruction pipelines.

Scaling training data to a diverse mixture of 17 3D datasets and increasing model capacity (e.g., embedding dimensions up to 1024) significantly improves generalization and robustness in local feature matching.

Current standard benchmarks are saturated, underscoring the critical need for more challenging datasets like HardMatch to accurately measure progress and identify real-world failure modes in feature matching.
originalProblem: Local feature matching was perceived to have inherent limitations, leading to a shift in research focus towards detector-free and feed-forward methods.
Existing local feature matching models were typically trained on limited, mid-sized datasets, hindering generalization and robustness across diverse scenarios.
Standard benchmarks for feature matching were becoming saturated, making it difficult to meaningfully assess progress or identify remaining challenges for state-of-the-art methods.
results: LoMa-G achieved 54.3 mAA@10px on the new HardMatch dataset, outperforming the previous state-of-the-art sparse method (ALIKED+LightGlue) by 18.6 mAA and dense matchers like RoMa v2 (46.5 mAA).

LoMa models established new state-of-the-art performance across numerous benchmarks, including 73.4 mAA@10px on WxBS, 89.3% mAA on IMC 2022, and significant gains in relative pose estimation (e.g., 8.4 AUC@5° on MegaDepth).

Ablation studies confirmed that increased data scale (transitioning to the full 17-dataset mixture) and increased model capacity (embedding dimensions from 256 to 1024) were the primary drivers of performance improvements.
solution: The authors re-evaluated local feature matching by applying modern machine learning practices, including large-scale, diverse data mixtures, advanced training recipes, and increased model capacity and computational resources.
A diverse training dataset was curated from 17 3D datasets, encompassing wide baseline and optical flow scenarios, with some existing data re-processed to generate higher-quality ground truth.

A novel, manually annotated benchmark, HardMatch, was introduced, consisting of 1000 highly challenging image pairs to address benchmark saturation and evaluate performance in extreme matching scenarios.
summary.summary: The paper "LoMa: Local Feature Matching Revisited" re-examines local feature matching by applying modern data-driven techniques. It develops a family of descriptor-matcher models, LoMa, which achieve new state-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks, including a challenging new dataset called HardMatch, often surpassing current dense matching and feed-forward reconstruction methods.

title: LoMa: Local Feature Matching Revisited
